title: Sugarloaf Mountain

infobox.value: You can help **expand this article with text translated from [the corresponding article](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar "pt:Pão de Açúcar") in Portuguese**. *(July 2011)* Click [show] for important translation instructions.  * [View](https://translate.google.com/translate?&u=https%3A%2F%2Fpt.wikipedia.org%2Fwiki%2FP%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar&sl=pt&tl=en&prev=_t&hl=en) a machine-translated version of the Portuguese article. * Machine translation, like [DeepL](https://deepl.com) or [Google Translate](https://translate.google.com/), is a useful starting point for translations, but translators must revise errors as necessary and confirm that the translation is accurate, rather than simply copy-pasting machine-translated text into the English Wikipedia. * Do not translate text that appears unreliable or low-quality. If possible, verify the text with references provided in the foreign-language article. * You **must** provide [copyright attribution](/wiki/Wikipedia:Copying_within_Wikipedia "Wikipedia:Copying within Wikipedia") in the [edit summary](/wiki/Help:Edit_summary "Help:Edit summary") accompanying your translation by providing an [interlanguage link](/wiki/Help:Interlanguage_links "Help:Interlanguage links") to the source of your translation. A model attribution edit summary is `Content in this edit is translated from the existing Portuguese Wikipedia article at [[:pt:Pão de Açúcar]]; see its history for attribution.` * You may also add the template `{{Translated|pt|Pão de Açúcar}}` to the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Sugarloaf_Mountain "Talk:Sugarloaf Mountain"). * For more guidance, see [Wikipedia:Translation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Translation "Wikipedia:Translation").

A model attribution edit summary is `Content in this edit is translated from the existing Portuguese Wikipedia article at [[:pt:Pão de Açúcar]]; see its history for attribution.` * You may also add the template `{{Translated|pt|Pão de Açúcar}}` to the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Sugarloaf_Mountain "Talk:Sugarloaf Mountain"). * For more guidance, see [Wikipedia:Translation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Translation "Wikipedia:Translation"). |

([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Pão de Açúcar*, pronounced [[ˈpɐ̃w d(ʒi) aˈsukaʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a peak situated in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, on a [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") at the mouth of [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"). Rising 396 m (1,299 ft) above the harbor,[[1]](#cite_note-1) the peak is named for its resemblance to the traditional shape of concentrated refined [sugarloaf](/wiki/Sugarloaf "Sugarloaf"). It is known worldwide for its  [cable car](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Cable_Car "Sugarloaf Cable Car") and panoramic views of the city and beyond.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Peak in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

This article is about the mountain in Rio de Janeiro. For other mountains with the same name, see [List of mountains named Sugarloaf](/wiki/List_of_mountains_named_Sugarloaf "List of mountains named Sugarloaf").

"Pão de Açúcar" redirects here. For the city, see [Pão de Açúcar, Alagoas](/wiki/P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar,_Alagoas "Pão de Açúcar, Alagoas"). For the retailer, see [Grupo Pão de Açúcar](/wiki/Grupo_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar "Grupo Pão de Açúcar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Sugarloaf_Sunrise_2.jpg/250px-Sugarloaf_Sunrise_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sugarloaf_Sunrise_2.jpg)

Sunrise in Rio de Janeiro with Sugarloaf Mountain, as seen from [Tijuca Forest](/wiki/Tijuca_Forest "Tijuca Forest")

**Sugarloaf Mountain** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Pão de Açúcar*, pronounced [[ˈpɐ̃w d(ʒi) aˈsukaʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a peak situated in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, on a [peninsula](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") at the mouth of [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"). Rising 396 m (1,299 ft) above the harbor,[[1]](#cite_note-1) the peak is named for its resemblance to the traditional shape of concentrated refined [sugarloaf](/wiki/Sugarloaf "Sugarloaf"). It is known worldwide for its  [cable car](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Cable_Car "Sugarloaf Cable Car") and panoramic views of the city and beyond.

The mountain is one of several [monolithic](/wiki/Monolith "Monolith") [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") mountains that rise straight from the water's edge around [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Geologically, it is considered part of a family of steep-sided rock outcroppings known as [bornhardts](/wiki/Bornhardt "Bornhardt").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Migon2004-4)

The mountain is protected by the [Sugarloaf Mountain and Urca Hill Natural Monument](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain_and_Urca_Hill_Natural_Monument "Sugarloaf Mountain and Urca Hill Natural Monument"), created in 2006.
This became part of a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") declared by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 2012.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Origins of the name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sugarloaf_Mountain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins of the name")]

The name Sugarloaf was coined in the 16th century by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") during the heyday of [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") trade in Brazil due to sugar imports from the Portuguese conquest of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), according to historian [Vieira Fazenda](/w/index.php?title=Vieira_Fazenda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vieira Fazenda (page does not exist)"). Blocks of sugar were placed in conical molds made of [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") to be transported on ships. The form of the peak reminded them of the well-known resulting "[sugarloaf](/wiki/Sugarloaf "Sugarloaf")" shape, and the nickname has since been extended to be a general descriptor for formations of this kind.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Cable car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sugarloaf_Mountain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cable car")]

Main article: [Sugarloaf Cable Car](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Cable_Car "Sugarloaf Cable Car")

A glass-walled [cable car](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Cable_Car "Sugarloaf Cable Car") (*bondinho* or, more formally, *teleférico*), capable of holding 65 people, runs along a 1,400 m (4,600 ft) route between the peaks of Sugarloaf and Morro da [Urca](/wiki/Urca "Urca") every 20 minutes. The original cable car line was built in 1912, rebuilt around 1972–73, and rebuilt again in 2008. The cable car goes from a ground station, at the base of [Morro da Babilônia](/wiki/Morro_da_Babil%C3%B4nia "Morro da Babilônia"), to Morro da Urca and thence to Sugarloaf's summit.

### Reaching the summit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sugarloaf_Mountain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reaching the summit")]

To reach the summit, passengers take two cable cars. The first ascends to the shorter Morro da Urca, 220 m (722 ft) high. The second car ascends to Pão de Açúcar.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Swiss-made bubble-shaped cars offer passengers 360° views of the surrounding city.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The ascent takes three minutes.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sugarloaf_Mountain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Timeline")]

* 1907 – Brazilian engineer [Augusto Ferreira Ramos](/w/index.php?title=Augusto_Ferreira_Ramos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Augusto Ferreira Ramos (page does not exist)") had the idea of linking the hills through a path in the air.
* 1910 – The same engineer founded the Society of Sugar Loaf, and in the same year, the works were started. The project was commissioned in Germany and built by Brazilian workers. All parts were taken by climbing mountains or lifted by steel cables.
* 1912 – Opening of the cableway, the first in Brazil and the third of this kind worldwide;[[10]](#cite_note-10) the first cable cars were made of coated wood and were used for 61 years.
* 1973 – The current models of cars were put into operation. This increased the carrying capacity by almost ten times.
* 2009 – Inauguration of the next generation of cable cars that had already been purchased and are on display at the base of Red Beach
* 2020 – Closed in March[[11]](#cite_note-11) and reopened in August[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Rio_de_Janeiro_Panoramic_from_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_crop.jpg/1280px-Rio_de_Janeiro_Panoramic_from_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_de_Janeiro_Panoramic_from_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_crop.jpg "Panoramic view of Rio de Janeiro and Niterói (right) from Sugarloaf")

Panoramic view of Rio de Janeiro and [Niterói](/wiki/Niter%C3%B3i "Niterói") (right) from Sugarloaf

## Rock climbing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sugarloaf_Mountain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rock climbing")]

There are [rock climbing](/wiki/Rock_climbing "Rock climbing") routes on Sugarloaf that are mostly [multipitch](/wiki/Multi-pitch_climbing "Multi-pitch climbing") and are a mixture of [sport](/wiki/Sport_climbing "Sport climbing") and [trad](/wiki/Traditional_climbing "Traditional climbing").[[14]](#cite_note-14) There are also two other mountains in the area with technical rock climbing, [Morro da Babilônia](/wiki/Morro_da_Babil%C3%B4nia "Morro da Babilônia")[[15]](#cite_note-15) and Morro da Urca.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Together, they form one of the largest urban climbing areas in the world, with more than 270 routes, between 1 and 10 [pitches](/wiki/Pitch_(climbing) "Pitch (climbing)") long.

[Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20240617055257/https://www.mountainproject.com/area/107546364/morro-da-urca) from the original on 17 June 2024. Retrieved 11 December 2021.
